heading: TONUS TERTIUS
card_summary: This opening chapter of the Tonus Tertius (Caput I) addresses how the One (monas) unfolds into multiplicity, specifically the number twenty-seven. Zorzi follows Plato's Timaeus — mediated through Proclus and Chalcidius — in tracing the World Soul's harmonic structure through doublings and triplings of the monad up to 27, the cube of the first number (3). The interpreters Proclus and Chalcidius substitute six (the first perfect number) for unity, extending the series to 162, then fill the intervals with diapente, diatessaron, tone, semitone, and leimma harmonics. Zorzi also invokes the Hebrew concept of materia prima — called Hivili or Tohu by the Hebrews, Chaos by the poets, and Hyle by the Greeks — which precedes numerical creation just as the supreme monad precedes number, and notes that Moses wisely described this formless substrate before the work of creation.
essay:
## Central Argument

The opening chapter of the Tonus Tertius poses the cosmological question of how the One (monas) diffuses itself into multiplicity without losing its essential unity. Zorzi's answer is harmonic proportion: the monad extends itself through the ratios of Platonic musical harmony, arriving at the number twenty-seven — the cube of three — as the structural limit of this diffusion.

## The Platonic Framework: Timaeus and the Number 27

Zorzi grounds the discussion explicitly in the *Timaeus*, noting that Plato followed Timaeus Locrus in constructing the World Soul through a series of doublings and triplings from the monad:

> Plato Timaeum Locrum imitatus animae, et mundi fabricam describit per acceptam monadem, et per duplatas, triplatas, et quadriplatas proportiones usque ad viginti et septem, cubum videlicet primi numeri devenit, addens omnia intervalla repleta fuisse.

<cite>p. 410</cite>

The series 1–2–3–4–9–8–27 (the Platonic lambda) yields 27 as the cube of 3, identified here as "cubum primi numeri" — the cube of the first (odd) number. This is the structural scaffolding of both the World Soul and the cosmos as a whole.

## Proclus and Chalcidius: The Senarius as Substitute Unity

Zorzi then turns to the major ancient commentators on the *Timaeus* — Proclus and Chalcidius, along with later interpreters ("posteri") — who make a technical substitution: instead of beginning the harmonic series from 1 (the monad itself), they place six (the first perfect number) in the position of unity:

> senarium primum perfectum numerum unitatis loco collocant, quem eodem ordine duplando, triplandoque usque ad 162 deveniunt. Et tunc intervalla replent diapentica, diatessaronica harmonia, tono etiam, semitonio, et lemmate, ut tota anima, totusque mundus harmonicus sit, et omnem contineat divinam harmoniam.

<cite>p. 410</cite>

This produces the extended series culminating in 162, which is then filled with the standard intervals of Greek musical theory: the diapente (fifth), diatessaron (fourth), the tone (whole step), the semitone, and the leimma (the small Pythagorean remainder). The goal is a fully harmonic cosmos — "tota anima, totusque mundus harmonicus" — in which every level of being participates in divine proportion.

## The Ternary and the Nine

Zorzi introduces a compressed cosmological schema based on the number three:

> Nam primus, et summus monas in primum numerum, ternarium videlicet diffunditur, qui si quadretur novenarium complet. Quo numero intellectuum separatorum ordines, coeli, elementariaque omnia summa harmonia distribuuntur.

<cite>p. 410–411</cite>

The supreme monad first projects into three (the ternary, the "primum numerum"), which when squared yields nine. This ninefold structure organizes the orders of separated intellects (angels), the heavens, and the elemental world — a preview of distributions Zorzi promises to demonstrate more fully below. When the nine is further cubed, it returns to the twenty-seven already established. The progression 1 → 3 → 9 → 27 encodes the entire cosmological hierarchy in a single arithmetic movement.

## Materia Prima: Chaos, Hyle, Tohu

Before the harmonic distribution of things can proceed, Zorzi introduces the concept of materia prima — the formless substrate that logically precedes creation. In a characteristic trilingual move, he gives this concept in three traditions:

> materia prima (quam poetae Chaos, Graeci Hyle, et Hebraei [Hivili vel Tohu] id est Hivili vel Tohu dicunt) praeexigebatur, quae cum numeratis non connumeraretur, sicut agens primum, et summus monas in aliorum numerum non venit, quamvis per unitatem suam omnibus praestet, ut numerentur.

<cite>p. 411</cite>

The equation is precise: materia prima stands to the created numerical order exactly as the supreme monad stands above all number. Neither is "counted among" the things it makes possible. The poets call it Chaos; the Greeks call it Hyle (matter); the Hebrew term given — rendered in transliterated form as "Hivili" and "Tohu" — corresponds to the biblical *tohu wa-bohu* of Genesis 1:2, the formless void before creation. The Hebrew term carries the technical kabbalistic resonance of unformed potentiality.

## Moses and the Wisdom of Genesis

The section closes with a brief but pointed observation: Moses, described as "sapientissime," described this formless matter *before* the account of creation — implicitly aligning the Mosaic text with the philosophical insight that an unformed substrate must precede all numerical and harmonic differentiation. This move harmonizes Platonic cosmology with the Hebrew scriptural tradition, a signature gesture of Zorzi's Christian Kabbalist synthesis.

## Significance for Zorzi's Larger Project

This chapter establishes the arithmetic and harmonic backbone of the Tonus Tertius. The movement from monad to ternary to nine to twenty-seven provides the quantitative skeleton onto which all subsequent distributions — of angelic orders, celestial spheres, elements, and created beings — will be mapped. The chapter also performs Zorzi's characteristic method: Plato and his Greek commentators (Proclus, Chalcidius) are read as corroborating both the Mosaic account and the kabbalistic understanding of pre-creational formlessness. The technical musical vocabulary (diapentica, diatessaronica, tonus, semitonium, lemma) anchors the metaphysics in precise Pythagorean proportionality, making the cosmos literally a harmonic structure.
